text: The laparoscopic view reveals a normal-sized uterus, bilateral ovarian endometriomas, and complete obliteration of the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon injects diluted vasopressin into the uterus and both ovarian endometriomas, using a dilution of ten units in one hundred milliliters of normal saline.
The surgeon facilitates the avascular plane of dissection during ovarian cystectomy, focusing on minimizing vascular involvement in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies contraction and counter traction techniques to conserve the underlying ovarian follicles, preserving the pool of follicles within the ovarian tissue.
The surgeon performs conservative dissection using blunt and sharp techniques with gold scissors, avoiding any energy sources, to separate tissue layers around the ovarian endometriomas.

The surgeon clears dense adhesions from the pouch of Douglas, revealing both ovarian endometriomas. The area is visibly freed from obstructions, allowing better visualization of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon performs an ovarian cystectomy on the left ovary, utilizing traction and counter traction techniques to carefully separate the cyst from surrounding tissue without the use of energy sources.
The surgeon clears the pouch of Douglas (POD) of thick, dense adhesions using traction, counter traction, cold scissor dissection, and blunt dissection, avoiding the use of energy sources.
The surgeon drains the ovarian cyst on the right side, employing traction and counter traction techniques to perform cystic knee dissection.
The surgeon drains the cyst to reveal the cyst wall, taking care to avoid unnecessary dissection, particularly urethrolysis and retroperitoneal dissection, as these areas are not visibly involved.
Saline irrigation is continuously applied to the surgical field to keep the tissue hydrated and prevent damage to the peritoneal mesothelial cells during the adhesiolysis procedure.
After completing two cystectomies on the right side, the surgeon reveals a third cyst and performs a cystectomy in the appropriate dissection plane.
The surgeon performs an ovarian cystectomy without using any energy source, carefully preserving the ovarian follicles. Bilateral fimbrioplasty is conducted to restore the fimbriae's function.
The surgeon releases significant adhesions between the left sigmoid colon and the lateral pelvic wall, carefully separating the structures to restore mobility.
